metadata.description: Comprehensive dream symbols knowledge base for AI dream interpretation

academic: Hall & Van de Castle (1966) - Content Analysis of Dreams
Mathes, Schredl & Goritz (2014) - Frequency of Typical Dream Themes
Domhoff (2003) - The Scientific Study of Dreams
Hill (2004) - Cognitive-Experiential Model of Dream Interpretation
cultural: Zhou Gong Jie Meng (周公解梦) - Duke of Zhou's Dream Dictionary
Ibn Sirin (653-729 CE) - Islamic Dream Interpretation
Agni Purana Chapter 229 - Significance of Dreams

metadata.frequency_scale: 1-10 (10 = most commonly reported worldwide, based on Typical Dreams Questionnaire data)

common_variations: Chased by monster or supernatural entity

psychological_interpretations.jungian: The pursuer often represents the Shadow archetype - repressed aspects of the self that demand integration. The dreamer is running from parts of themselves they refuse to acknowledge. The specific form of the pursuer reveals the nature of the denied quality.
psychological_interpretations.freudian: Expression of repressed anxiety or guilt. The pursuer symbolizes a forbidden desire or traumatic memory the ego is trying to avoid. The act of fleeing represents defense mechanisms at work.
psychological_interpretations.cognitive: Threat Simulation Theory: the brain rehearses fight-or-flight responses to prepare for real-world dangers. Continuity Hypothesis: reflects waking-life avoidance behaviors - the dreamer is running from a problem, confrontation, or responsibility they are avoiding while awake.

cultural_interpretations.western: Generally indicates avoidance of a problem, fear of confrontation, or anxiety about something catching up to you. The identity of the pursuer matters - known person suggests relationship conflict; unknown figure suggests internal fears.
cultural_interpretations.chinese: Being chased (被追赶) in 周公解梦 can indicate that the dreamer is under pressure from competitors or adversaries. If you escape, it suggests overcoming difficulties. If caught, it may warn of impending trouble that needs direct attention.
cultural_interpretations.islamic: In Ibn Sirin's interpretation, being chased may represent fleeing from one's responsibilities or duties. If chased by a known person, it may indicate unresolved conflict. Being chased by an animal may represent one's nafs (lower desires) pursuing them.
cultural_interpretations.hindu: In Swapna Shastra, being pursued can indicate karmic debts coming due or unresolved dharmic obligations. The nature of the pursuer indicates the nature of the karmic lesson.

follow_up_questions: Who or what was chasing you? Could you see them clearly?
How did you feel during the chase - terrified, annoyed, or something else?
Did you escape, get caught, or did it just keep going?
Is there something in your waking life you've been avoiding or putting off?
Where were you running - a familiar place or somewhere unknown?
Have you had this dream before? When did it start?

common_variations: Falling from a building or cliff
Falling into a void or darkness
Falling and waking up with a jolt (hypnic jerk)

psychological_interpretations.jungian: Symbolizes a loss of psychic grounding - the dreamer has become inflated (too identified with Persona) or is losing connection to the Self. It can also represent the necessary descent into the unconscious for growth.
psychological_interpretations.freudian: Represents anxiety about loss of control, fear of failure, or sexual surrender. Freud also linked falling dreams to childhood memories of being lifted and dropped playfully.
psychological_interpretations.cognitive: Physiological trigger: hypnic myoclonia (muscle spasms during sleep onset) can cause the brain to construct a falling narrative. Psychologically, reflects real feelings of losing grip - on a situation, relationship, or sense of stability.

cultural_interpretations.western: Classic anxiety symbol - losing control, fear of failure, or feeling unsupported. Often occurs during transitions (new job, relationship change, moving). The height of the fall may reflect the magnitude of the perceived risk.
cultural_interpretations.chinese: 周公解梦: Falling from a height (从高处坠落) generally warns of setbacks in career or relationships. Falling into water may indicate upcoming financial difficulties. However, falling and landing safely can indicate recovering from difficulties.
cultural_interpretations.islamic: Falling from a height may symbolize a decrease in status or faith. Ibn Sirin notes that falling into a known place can indicate returning to old circumstances, while falling into the unknown warns of risk. Falling and being caught indicates divine protection.
cultural_interpretations.hindu: In Swapna Shastra, falling is generally considered inauspicious. It may indicate obstacles in one's path, loss of social standing, or spiritual decline. The time of night the dream occurs affects its significance.

follow_up_questions: Where were you falling from?
Did you land, or did you wake up before impact?
Were you pushed, or did you slip?
How did you feel while falling - panicked, peaceful, or resigned?
Is there an area of your life where you feel things are slipping out of control?
Did anyone else see you fall? Were they trying to help?

common_variations: Teeth falling out one by one
All teeth falling out at once

psychological_interpretations.jungian: Teeth represent power, vitality, and the ability to 'bite into life.' Loss of teeth symbolizes a loss of personal power or a transitional period where the old self is being shed for a new one - similar to how children lose baby teeth for adult ones.
psychological_interpretations.freudian: Freud associated teeth dreams with castration anxiety and repressed sexual anxieties. Also linked to concerns about physical appearance and aging. The mouth as an erogenous zone connects to oral fixation.
psychological_interpretations.cognitive: Research by Rozen & Soffer-Dudek (2018) found correlations between teeth dreams and actual dental irritation during sleep (teeth grinding/bruxism). Also linked to waking concerns about appearance, speech, competence, and personal power.

cultural_interpretations.western: Commonly associated with vanity/appearance concerns, fear of aging, powerlessness, embarrassment, or fear of being judged. Can also relate to communication difficulties - literally 'losing your bite.'
cultural_interpretations.chinese: 周公解梦: Teeth falling out (梦见掉牙) traditionally relates to family health - specifically, upper teeth represent elders and lower teeth represent younger family members. If blood is present, it amplifies the warning. However, some interpretations see it as a sign of positive transformation.
cultural_interpretations.islamic: In Ibn Sirin's interpretation, teeth represent family members - upper teeth for male relatives, lower for female. Losing a tooth may indicate the illness or death of the corresponding family member. Teeth falling into one's hand can mean receiving money.
cultural_interpretations.hindu: In Swapna Shastra, dreaming of losing teeth can indicate upcoming financial loss or family discord. However, dreaming of clean white teeth being lost may indicate gaining wisdom through loss.

follow_up_questions: How did you feel when your teeth fell out - panicked, embarrassed, or calm?
Did all teeth fall out or just specific ones?
Were other people around when it happened? Did they notice?
Have you been stressed about your appearance or how others perceive you?
Is there a situation where you feel you've lost your 'power' or voice?
Do you grind your teeth at night? Any recent dental issues?

common_variations: Flying but struggling to stay airborne
Flying in an aircraft that changes to self-powered flight

psychological_interpretations.jungian: Transcendence of the ego, connection to the spiritual Self. Can represent inflation (flying too high = Icarus complex) or individuation - rising above limited perspectives to see the whole. The ease of flight indicates the dreamer's relationship with their higher aspirations.
psychological_interpretations.freudian: Freud interpreted flying as disguised sexual pleasure and arousal. Also represents wish fulfillment - the desire to rise above one's current situation, escape limitations, or achieve forbidden goals.
psychological_interpretations.cognitive: Often accompanies lucid dreaming. Reflects feelings of liberation, mastery, ambition, or creative breakthrough. Difficulty flying correlates with waking feelings of being held back. Brain may simulate vestibular sensations during REM.

cultural_interpretations.western: Freedom, ambition, perspective, transcendence. Flying easily = feeling empowered and in control. Struggling to fly = aspirations blocked by doubt or obstacles. Flying away from something = escapism.
cultural_interpretations.chinese: 周公解梦: Flying (梦见飞翔) over mountains suggests overcoming obstacles and desire for freedom. Flying high indicates ambition and upward career trajectory. Falling during flight warns of overreaching or pride before a setback.
cultural_interpretations.islamic: Flying in a dream usually symbolizes ambition, freedom, and spiritual elevation. Ibn Sirin notes that flying from one's home may indicate travel. Flying without wings indicates a change in circumstances. Flying to Mecca indicates spiritual achievement.
cultural_interpretations.hindu: In Swapna Shastra, flying dreams are generally auspicious, symbolizing spiritual elevation and transcendence of material concerns. The lotus, a key Hindu symbol, grows from mud to float above water - similarly, the flying dreamer rises above worldly troubles.

follow_up_questions: How did you feel while flying - exhilarated, scared, or struggling?
Were you flying freely or were there obstacles?
How high were you flying? What could you see below?
Were you flying toward something or away from something?
Is there an area in your life where you feel liberated or want to break free?
Did you have control of the flight, or was it happening to you?

common_variations: Sex with a known person (not partner)

psychological_interpretations.jungian: Often represents the union of opposites - the merging of Anima/Animus. Sex with an unknown figure suggests integration of unconscious qualities. The identity of the partner reveals which psychic qualities the dreamer is trying to unite with.
psychological_interpretations.freudian: Wish fulfillment of repressed sexual desires. The manifest content disguises latent desires through symbolism. Partners may represent forbidden objects of desire. The specific acts reveal the nature of the repressed material.
psychological_interpretations.cognitive: Continuity of waking sexual thoughts and desires. Also reflects needs for intimacy, connection, validation, or power. The partner chosen often reflects qualities the dreamer admires or desires to embody rather than literal sexual attraction.

cultural_interpretations.western: Desire for intimacy, merging of qualities, creative union, power dynamics. Sex with a stranger = desire to discover unknown aspects of self. Sex with an authority figure = desire for power or approval.
cultural_interpretations.chinese: 周公解梦 interprets sexual dreams contextually. Dreaming of intimacy may indicate harmonious relationships or upcoming good fortune in partnerships. Illicit encounters may warn of temptation or moral choices ahead.
cultural_interpretations.islamic: Ibn Sirin notes that sexual dreams can fall into the category of Hulm (from Shaytan) or Hadith al-Nafs (daily thoughts). Specific interpretations depend heavily on the partner, context, and the dreamer's marital status. Generally, appropriate sexual dreams may indicate marriage or prosperity.
cultural_interpretations.hindu: Swapna Shastra treats sexual dreams as natural expressions of desire (kama). The partner and context determine interpretation. Dreams of divine union (as with Krishna and Radha) represent spiritual longing and devotion.

follow_up_questions: Who was the other person in the dream? Someone you know?
What emotions did you feel - pleasure, guilt, confusion?
Is this a recurring dream or a one-time occurrence?
Are there qualities in that person you admire or want to develop in yourself?
How is intimacy and connection in your waking life right now?
Was the experience consensual and positive, or uncomfortable?

common_variations: Back in school as an adult
Taking an exam in the wrong subject

psychological_interpretations.jungian: The examination represents an inner test of competence - a confrontation with the Self's demands for growth. The school setting represents the psyche's learning process. Failing indicates the dreamer feels they haven't met an internal standard.
psychological_interpretations.freudian: Linked to performance anxiety and fear of judgment. The examiner represents a parental or authority figure whose approval is sought. Being unprepared reflects childhood fears of inadequacy resurfacing.
psychological_interpretations.cognitive: Strong support for Continuity Hypothesis - exam dreams often occur before evaluative situations (job interviews, presentations, deadlines). The brain rehearses worst-case scenarios. Persists into adulthood because school was the formative context for performance evaluation.

cultural_interpretations.western: Universal anxiety about being evaluated, judged, or found lacking. Common among high achievers and perfectionists. Often surfaces before major life evaluations even decades after leaving school.
cultural_interpretations.chinese: 周公解梦: Dreaming of taking exams (梦见考试) is generally auspicious, suggesting the dreamer will succeed in their endeavors. However, dreaming of failing an exam may indicate self-doubt that needs to be addressed. In Chinese culture where 高考 shapes lives, exam dreams carry intense significance.
cultural_interpretations.islamic: Exams in dreams may represent tests of faith or character. Ibn Sirin associates passing an exam with overcoming a trial in life. Failing may indicate a need for more preparation in one's spiritual or worldly duties.
cultural_interpretations.hindu: In Vedic tradition, being tested in dreams relates to karmic trials. Success indicates progress on one's dharmic path, while failure suggests the need for more self-work and spiritual discipline.

follow_up_questions: What was the exam about? Was it a subject you actually studied?
Were you unprepared, or did you suddenly forget everything?
Is there a situation in your waking life where you feel evaluated or tested?
How long ago were you actually in school? Do you dream about school often?
Who else was there - classmates, a teacher, an authority figure?
Did you finish the exam? What was the outcome?

common_variations: A dead person speaking to you

psychological_interpretations.jungian: Death symbolizes the necessary ending of an old attitude, belief system, or phase of life for the emergence of something new. A crucial stage of individuation. The death of a specific person may represent the death of the quality they embody in the dreamer's psyche.
psychological_interpretations.freudian: Can represent repressed death wishes toward the person who dies (especially family members), fear of one's own mortality, or anxiety about loss. Also connects to the death drive (Thanatos) - the pull toward dissolution.
psychological_interpretations.cognitive: Threat simulation rehearsal for processing grief and mortality. Also reflects major life transitions - career changes, relationship endings, identity shifts. The brain metaphorically codes endings as death. Often more about transformation than literal fear.

cultural_interpretations.western: Transformation, ending of a chapter, rebirth. Rarely literal. Dying in a dream may indicate a part of your personality or life situation that is changing. Someone else dying may reflect changing feelings about them or what they represent.
cultural_interpretations.chinese: 周公解梦: Paradoxically, dreaming of one's own death (梦见自己死) is often considered auspicious - suggesting longevity, wealth, or positive transformation. Dreaming of a deceased person speaking to you may carry an important message. Death of family members may indicate they will have long life.
cultural_interpretations.islamic: Ibn Sirin differentiates: dreaming of one's own death without typical funeral rituals can indicate longevity. A dead person speaking indicates truth, as the dead cannot lie. Washing a dead person indicates helping someone repent. Death dreams carry specific meanings based on who dies and how.
cultural_interpretations.hindu: In Swapna Shastra, dreaming of death is often considered auspicious - it may signify transformation, liberation (moksha), or the end of suffering. Seeing dead relatives may indicate their blessings or messages from the ancestral realm (pitru loka).

follow_up_questions: Who died in the dream - you, or someone else?
How did the death occur? Was it peaceful or violent?
How did you feel - grief, relief, fear, or acceptance?
Is there a major change or ending happening in your waking life?
If someone else died, what do they represent to you?
Did you see what happened after death? Was there an afterlife scene?

symbols.frequency_pct: N/A - appears in ~35% of all dream reports (DreamBank)

psychological_interpretations.jungian: Water is the primary symbol of the unconscious mind itself. Its state reflects the dreamer's relationship to their unconscious - calm water = harmony with deeper self; turbulent water = emotional upheaval or unprocessed material surfacing. Diving underwater = actively exploring the unconscious.
psychological_interpretations.freudian: Water represents birth (amniotic fluid), sexuality, and the id. Immersion in water symbolizes regression to prenatal states. Turbulent water represents uncontrollable desires.
psychological_interpretations.cognitive: Emotional barometer - water maps directly to the dreamer's emotional state. Clear and calm = emotional clarity. Murky = confusion. Rising/flooding = feeling overwhelmed. The type and movement of water precisely mirrors current emotional conditions.

cultural_interpretations.western: Emotions, the unconscious, purification, life flow. The clarity and behavior of water tells the emotional story. Swimming competently = navigating emotions well. Drowning = overwhelmed.
cultural_interpretations.chinese: 周公解梦: Water (水) is generally auspicious, representing wealth and prosperity. Clear water = smooth fortune. Muddy water = incoming difficulties. Flood water = challenges but also potential for great change. Drinking clean water = good health.
cultural_interpretations.islamic: Water represents knowledge, life, and purification in Islamic interpretation. Clear water = beneficial knowledge. Muddy water = hardship. Drinking pure water = gaining righteous knowledge. Drowning may indicate being overcome by worldly affairs.
cultural_interpretations.hindu: Water is sacred (jal) and represents purification, life force (prana), and emotional flow. Rivers like the Ganges in dreams signify spiritual cleansing. Bathing in clear water is auspicious. Floods may represent the dissolution cycle of Brahma.

follow_up_questions: What was the water like - calm, turbulent, clear, or murky?
Were you in the water, observing it, or being affected by it?
How did you feel around the water - peaceful, terrified, refreshed?
Were you able to breathe underwater?
Is there an emotional situation in your life that feels overwhelming or cleansing?
Was anyone else with you near the water?

psychological_interpretations.jungian: One of the most powerful archetypal symbols. Represents transformation (shedding skin), healing (the caduceus), kundalini energy, the chthonic unconscious, and instinctual wisdom. Can represent the Shadow or the devouring aspect of the Great Mother.
psychological_interpretations.freudian: Classic phallic symbol representing repressed sexual energy, male sexuality, or fear of sexuality. Also represents fear, danger, and hidden threats lurking in the unconscious.
psychological_interpretations.cognitive: Evolutionary psychology: humans have a prepared fear response to snakes (Ohman & Mineka, 2003). Snake dreams may activate innate threat detection circuits. Also represents hidden dangers, deception, or things lurking beneath the surface of awareness.

cultural_interpretations.western: Transformation, hidden danger, healing, temptation (Eden narrative), sexuality, wisdom, betrayal. Context determines valence - a snake shedding skin = positive transformation; a snake striking = hidden threat.
cultural_interpretations.chinese: 周公解梦: Snakes (蛇) have complex meanings. Dreaming of a snake can indicate wealth and good fortune. Being bitten may mean unexpected wealth but also warns of enemies. A snake entering the house may indicate financial gain. White snakes are particularly auspicious. A snake in the grass warns of hidden enemies.
cultural_interpretations.islamic: Ibn Sirin: Snakes generally represent enemies or hidden dangers. Killing a snake = overcoming an enemy. Being bitten = being harmed by an enemy. A snake entering one's home = an enemy among household members. However, a friendly snake can represent a helper.
cultural_interpretations.hindu: Snakes (Naga) hold deep sacred significance. Dreaming of snakes can represent Kundalini energy awakening, divine protection (Lord Shiva wears Vasuki), or upcoming transformation. A cobra spreading its hood may indicate divine protection. Snake worship (Nag Panchami) reflects this reverence.

follow_up_questions: What kind of snake was it? Color, size, species?
Did it bite you or threaten you, or was it passive?
Where did you encounter the snake - inside or outside?
How did you feel - afraid, fascinated, or calm?
Is there a situation in your life that feels hidden or threatening?
Is there something in your life going through a transformation?

psychological_interpretations.jungian: The Persona (social mask) has been stripped away, revealing the true self. Can be terrifying (exposure of vulnerability) or liberating (authenticity). If others don't notice, it suggests the dreamer's anxiety about exposure is self-generated.
psychological_interpretations.freudian: Exhibitionist desires from childhood resurfacing. The conflict between desire to be seen and shame about being seen. Reflects anxieties about sexual exposure or body image.
psychological_interpretations.cognitive: Social anxiety manifested physically. Reflects fear of exposure, vulnerability, imposter syndrome, or being unprepared. Common before public presentations or new social situations. If nobody notices, the brain may be processing that fears are overblown.

cultural_interpretations.western: Vulnerability, exposure, authenticity, shame, imposter syndrome. Very common before job interviews, presentations, or entering new social groups. The reaction of others in the dream is key to interpretation.
cultural_interpretations.chinese: 周公解梦: Being naked (梦见裸体) in front of others may indicate upcoming embarrassment or loss of face (丢脸). However, some interpretations suggest it means the dreamer will receive honest feedback. Context of who sees the nudity matters.
cultural_interpretations.islamic: Nudity in dreams may represent exposure of sins or secrets. However, if the dreamer is naked in private (alone), it may not carry negative meaning. Public nudity warns of scandal or loss of reputation. Context and audience are crucial.
cultural_interpretations.hindu: In Swapna Shastra, nakedness can represent spiritual purity and renunciation (as with Jain monks) or vulnerability. The context determines whether it is liberation from material attachment or exposure of weakness.

follow_up_questions: Where were you when you realized you were naked/underdressed?
Did other people notice? How did they react?
How did you feel - mortified, indifferent, or free?
Is there a situation where you feel exposed or like a fraud?
Are you entering a new social situation or role?
Were you trying to cover up or were you comfortable?

symbols.frequency_pct: N/A - most common single symbol in DreamBank corpus (top #1 symbol)
common_variations: Unfamiliar house that feels like home

psychological_interpretations.jungian: The house represents the psyche itself. Different floors represent different levels of consciousness: attic = higher mind/spirit; main floor = conscious ego; basement = unconscious/Shadow. Discovering new rooms = discovering new aspects of self. A decaying house = neglected psyche.
psychological_interpretations.freudian: The house represents the body. Doors and windows represent body openings. The facade represents the social self. Specific rooms have sexual significance. The house's condition reflects the dreamer's relationship with their body.
psychological_interpretations.cognitive: Cognitive mapping of self-concept. The house represents one's life structure - career, relationships, identity. Renovations = life changes. Decay = neglected areas of life. New rooms = untapped potential. Intruders = boundary violations.

cultural_interpretations.western: The self, one's life, security, identity. Childhood home = past/nostalgia/unresolved issues. New rooms = undiscovered potential. Damage = areas of life falling apart.
cultural_interpretations.chinese: 周公解梦: Dreaming of a big house (大房子) indicates prosperity and expanding fortune. Building a house suggests achievement. A collapsing house warns of family troubles. Buying a new house indicates positive changes ahead.
cultural_interpretations.islamic: The house represents the dreamer's life and family. A spacious house = ease and comfort. A narrow house = difficulty. Building a house = building one's faith. A strange house = the grave or afterlife.
cultural_interpretations.hindu: Houses in dreams represent the body-temple (deha). A well-maintained house indicates dharmic living. Discovering new rooms suggests spiritual awakening. A crumbling house may indicate health concerns.

follow_up_questions: Was it your actual home, childhood home, or an unfamiliar place?
What was the condition of the house?
Did you discover any new rooms or hidden spaces?
Were you alone or with others? Who was there?
What was the feeling - comfortable, eerie, exciting?
Is there an area of your life that feels like it needs renovation or attention?

common_variations: Late for a wedding (own or others')

psychological_interpretations.jungian: Time pressure represents the tension between the ego's agenda and the Self's deeper timing. The dreamer may be out of sync with their own individuation process - trying to meet external deadlines while the inner self demands something different.
psychological_interpretations.freudian: Fear of missing out on opportunities, anxiety about deadlines and obligations. Can represent reluctance toward the event you're late for - lateness as unconscious resistance.
psychological_interpretations.cognitive: Directly reflects waking-life time anxiety and fear of consequences. The brain rehearses worst-case scenarios around scheduling. Strong Continuity Hypothesis support - these dreams spike during busy/stressful periods.

cultural_interpretations.western: Time anxiety, fear of missing opportunities, feeling overwhelmed by obligations, fear of disappointing others. Very common in fast-paced, deadline-driven cultures.
cultural_interpretations.chinese: 周公解梦: Being late (迟到) suggests the dreamer may be missing opportunities in waking life. Missing transportation indicates a need to reassess timing in business or personal decisions.
cultural_interpretations.islamic: Being late may indicate procrastination in religious duties or worldly obligations. Missing a journey could indicate missed opportunities for spiritual growth.
cultural_interpretations.hindu: Lateness in dreams may reflect anxieties about dharmic timing (the right moment for action). In Vedic thought, timing (muhurta) is sacred, and being late suggests misalignment with cosmic rhythm.

follow_up_questions: What were you late for?
What was preventing you from getting there on time?
How did you feel - panicked, frustrated, or resigned?
Is there an important deadline or commitment you're worried about?
Do you feel overwhelmed by your schedule in waking life?
Did you eventually arrive, or did you never make it?

common_variations: Stuck in place while danger approaches

psychological_interpretations.jungian: Represents a state of psychological paralysis - the ego is confronted by the overwhelming power of the unconscious and cannot act. May indicate a period where the dreamer needs to surrender control rather than fight.
psychological_interpretations.freudian: Conflict between desire (to act) and prohibition (superego). The paralysis represents the successful repression of a forbidden impulse. Also represents helplessness in the face of anxiety.
psychological_interpretations.cognitive: Physiological component: during REM sleep, the body experiences natural muscle atonia (paralysis). If the dreamer becomes semi-conscious during REM, they may experience this as inability to move. Psychologically reflects feelings of powerlessness in waking situations.

cultural_interpretations.western: Powerlessness, helplessness, inability to act in a critical moment. Often reflects real feelings of being trapped in an untenable situation. Physical component (sleep paralysis) adds a physiological dimension.
cultural_interpretations.chinese: 周公解梦: Being unable to move (鬼压床 - 'ghost pressing the bed') is attributed to supernatural influence. Traditionally, it's seen as a visitation by a spirit or ghost. Protective rituals may be recommended.
cultural_interpretations.islamic: Paralysis in sleep (especially with a sense of presence) may be attributed to jinn. Islamic practice recommends reciting specific surahs for protection. The experience is acknowledged as real but managed through spiritual practice.
cultural_interpretations.hindu: Sleep paralysis may be understood as the astral body not fully reconnecting with the physical body. It can also indicate spiritual awakening experiences or encounters with subtle realm beings.

follow_up_questions: Were you trying to run, fight, or call for help?
Was there something threatening nearby?
Did you feel a presence in the room?
Were you between sleep and waking (sleep paralysis)?
Is there a situation in your waking life where you feel powerless or stuck?
How often does this happen?

symbols.name_en: Being Lost / Lost in Unknown Place
common_variations: Lost in a forest or wilderness
Lost in a building (hospital, school, mall)
Others can't help you find the way

psychological_interpretations.jungian: Being lost in the wilderness represents the night sea journey - a necessary period of disorientation in the individuation process. The ego has lost its familiar landmarks and must find new orientation from the Self rather than the Persona.
psychological_interpretations.freudian: Anxiety about identity and direction in life. Being lost symbolizes loss of the ego's organizing function - the dreamer cannot find their way because they've lost contact with their desires and drives.
psychological_interpretations.cognitive: Spatial disorientation maps to life disorientation. Reflects real feelings of being directionless, confused about decisions, or uncertain about the future. Common during major life transitions.

cultural_interpretations.western: Confusion about life direction, identity crisis, feeling overwhelmed by choices, disconnection from purpose. The landscape where you're lost provides clues - city = social confusion; nature = spiritual seeking; building = institutional/career confusion.
cultural_interpretations.chinese: 周公解梦: Being lost (迷路) indicates confusion in decision-making. It may suggest the dreamer is at a crossroads and needs guidance. Finding the way eventually is a good omen for resolution.
cultural_interpretations.islamic: Being lost may represent going astray from the straight path (sirat al-mustaqim). It can indicate spiritual confusion or the need for guidance. Finding a guide in the dream is highly auspicious.
cultural_interpretations.hindu: Being lost represents maya (illusion) obscuring the path of dharma. The dreamer is caught in the web of worldly confusion and needs spiritual clarity. A guru appearing to guide represents divine grace.

follow_up_questions: Where were you lost - a city, nature, or a building?
Were you alone or with others?
Were you trying to get somewhere specific, or just trying to find any way out?
Are there major decisions or directions you're uncertain about in waking life?
Did you eventually find your way, or did the dream end while lost?
How did you feel - panicked, curious, or resigned?

symbols.frequency_pct: N/A - #3 most common symbol in DreamBank

psychological_interpretations.jungian: The vehicle represents the vessel carrying the ego through life. Who is driving = who is in control of your life direction. The condition of the vehicle = the state of your personal resources and energy. A breakdown = a psychic breakdown or exhaustion.
psychological_interpretations.freudian: The car is a phallic symbol representing power, control, and sexual drive. Speed = libidinal energy. Loss of control = loss of impulse control. Accidents = self-destructive tendencies or collision with reality.
psychological_interpretations.cognitive: Direct metaphor for life control and direction. Driving = agency in your own life. Passenger = others controlling your direction. Brakes failing = inability to stop something in your life. Reflects everyday driving anxiety and life navigation concerns.

cultural_interpretations.western: Control, direction, ambition, personal power. The type of vehicle reflects self-image. Who's driving reflects who controls your life decisions. Road conditions reflect life circumstances.
cultural_interpretations.chinese: 周公解梦: Cars (汽车) represent one's career journey and social status. A smooth drive = smooth career progress. A car accident warns of obstacles or health issues. Buying a new car indicates upward mobility.
cultural_interpretations.islamic: Vehicles represent one's life journey and the means by which one travels through it. A smooth ride = ease in life. Difficulty driving = trials ahead. The destination matters for interpretation.
cultural_interpretations.hindu: The chariot (ratha) is a powerful Vedic symbol - the Katha Upanishad describes the body as a chariot, the soul as its passenger, intellect as the driver, and the senses as horses. Vehicle dreams reflect this relationship between the soul and its worldly journey.

follow_up_questions: Were you driving, or was someone else?
What was the condition of the vehicle?
Were you in control, or was the car out of control?
Where were you headed?
Do you feel in control of your life direction right now?
Were the brakes, steering, or any part malfunctioning?

common_variations: Trying to dial a phone number but keep mismdialing
Trying to pack but items keep multiplying
Trying to get dressed but clothes keep changing
Trying to write but can't form words
Trying to run but moving in slow motion
Trying to complete a task but obstacles keep arising

psychological_interpretations.jungian: Sisyphean repetition signals that the ego's approach is fundamentally wrong - the task cannot be completed with the current attitude. The Self is demanding a change in approach, not more effort.
psychological_interpretations.freudian: Frustration dreams reflect unsatisfied desires and the inability to achieve wish fulfillment. The repetitive failure represents the repeated thwarting of id impulses by superego restrictions.
psychological_interpretations.cognitive: Motor cortex activity during REM creates these movement distortions. Psychologically reflects waking feelings of futility, spinning wheels, or making no progress despite effort.

cultural_interpretations.western: Frustration, burnout, feeling stuck, perfectionism. Often indicates the dreamer is approaching a problem the wrong way and needs to change strategy rather than try harder.
cultural_interpretations.chinese: 周公解梦: Repeated failure may indicate that the dreamer is fighting against the natural flow (逆势). The advice is often to step back and reassess rather than push forward.
cultural_interpretations.islamic: Repeated failure in dreams may indicate the need for more patience (sabr) and trust in divine timing. It can also suggest the dreamer needs to seek help or change their approach.
cultural_interpretations.hindu: Relates to the concept of karma phala (fruit of action) - repeated failure suggests wrong action (adharma) or attachment to results. The Bhagavad Gita teaches action without attachment to outcome.

follow_up_questions: What were you trying to do in the dream?
What kept going wrong?
How did you feel - frustrated, anxious, or absurdly calm?
Is there a project or goal in waking life where you feel like you're spinning your wheels?
Did you eventually succeed, give up, or just keep trying?
Have you considered approaching the problem differently?

symbols.name_en: Dead Person Alive / Person Alive Dead
symbols.name_zh: 死者复活 / 活人死去
common_variations: Deceased parent or grandparent appearing alive
Knowing the person is dead but seeing them alive
Living person appearing dead in dream
Having a conversation with a dead person
Dead person looking healthy and happy

psychological_interpretations.jungian: The dead person represents an internalized psychic image (imago) that still influences the dreamer. Their appearance suggests unfinished psychological business or that the quality they represent is becoming active again in the psyche.
psychological_interpretations.freudian: Wish fulfillment - the dreamer wishes the person were still alive. Can also represent unresolved grief, guilt, or the need for closure. The message from the dead may represent the dreamer's own unexpressed thoughts.
psychological_interpretations.cognitive: Grief processing and memory consolidation. The brain replays and reworks emotional memories. These dreams often decrease in frequency over time but may spike during anniversaries or life events the deceased would have been part of.

cultural_interpretations.western: Unresolved grief, desire for guidance, keeping memory alive. The deceased person's demeanor matters - happy/healthy = the dreamer is processing grief well; sick/distressed = unresolved emotional issues.
cultural_interpretations.chinese: 周公解梦: Dreaming of a deceased person (梦见死人) is common and has specific interpretations. A dead person giving you money = good fortune. A dead person talking = listen carefully for a message. Eating with a dead person = longevity.
cultural_interpretations.islamic: Ibn Sirin: The dead cannot lie, so messages from deceased people in dreams are considered truthful. A dead person looking well-dressed indicates they are in a good state in the afterlife. Feeding the dead or being asked for items = charitable giving on their behalf (sadaqah) is needed.
cultural_interpretations.hindu: Ancestors (pitru) visiting in dreams is taken seriously. They may need prayers, rituals (shraddha), or offerings. A happy ancestor indicates they are at peace. A distressed ancestor needs prayer and pinda (ritual offerings).

follow_up_questions: Who was the person? What was your relationship?
How long ago did they pass away?
Did they say anything to you? What was the message?
How did they look - healthy, sick, happy, sad?
Is there something unresolved in your relationship with this person?
Is there a life decision where you wish you could ask their advice?

common_variations: Baby that is your younger self

psychological_interpretations.jungian: The Divine Child archetype - represents new beginnings, potential, innocence, and the emerging Self. A neglected baby = a neglected creative or spiritual potential. Finding a baby = discovering a new aspect of self. The child is the symbol of the Self's renewal.
psychological_interpretations.freudian: Wish for or anxiety about parenthood. Can represent the dreamer's own inner child and unmet childhood needs. A vulnerable baby reflects the dreamer's own vulnerability.
psychological_interpretations.cognitive: New projects, ideas, relationships, or phases of life that need nurturing and protection. A forgotten baby = anxiety about neglecting something important. Reflects actual parental concerns for parents.

cultural_interpretations.western: New beginnings, innocence, vulnerability, potential, responsibility. A healthy baby = positive new development. A sick baby = a struggling new venture. Forgetting a baby = neglecting something important.
cultural_interpretations.chinese: 周公解梦: Dreaming of a baby (梦见婴儿) is generally auspicious, indicating new opportunities and good fortune. A crying baby may indicate a need for attention to a neglected matter. A laughing baby = joy and prosperity ahead.
cultural_interpretations.islamic: Babies in dreams often represent blessings, joy, and good news. A beautiful baby = incoming blessings. Breastfeeding = nurturing faith. However, context matters - a distressed baby may indicate worries.
cultural_interpretations.hindu: In Swapna Shastra, children represent new beginnings and the creative force of Brahma. A divine child may represent the avatar principle - God incarnating in pure form. Dreaming of Lord Krishna as a child is extremely auspicious.

follow_up_questions: Was the baby yours, or did you find it?
How did you feel about the baby - protective, overwhelmed, or surprised?
Was the baby healthy and happy, or in distress?
Is there a new project, relationship, or phase of life you're nurturing?
Did anyone else help you with the baby?
Have you been thinking about parenthood or your own childhood?

psychological_interpretations.jungian: Fire is the symbol of transformation, passion, and the alchemical process of individuation (the philosopher's stone requires fire). Destructive fire = the burning away of the old self. Illuminating fire = consciousness and enlightenment. Fire is both creator and destroyer.
psychological_interpretations.freudian: Passion, anger, repressed rage, sexual desire ('burning with desire'). Fire also represents the id's destructive urges. Playing with fire = flirting with forbidden desires.
psychological_interpretations.cognitive: Intense emotions - anger, passion, anxiety, urgency. The type of fire maps to the type of emotional intensity. Controlled fire (hearth) = managed passion. Wildfire = emotions out of control. Threat simulation of a primal danger.

cultural_interpretations.western: Transformation, destruction, passion, anger, purification, creativity. A controlled fire = positive transformation. An out-of-control fire = dangerous emotions or situations.
cultural_interpretations.chinese: 周公解梦: Fire (火) has dual meanings. A big fire burning brightly = great prosperity and wealth ahead. Seeing fire from afar = success. But being burned = caution against reckless behavior. Fire in the kitchen = domestic harmony.
cultural_interpretations.islamic: Fire can represent fitna (trial), hellfire, or purification. A beneficial fire (for cooking/warmth) = positive, sustenance. A destructive fire = warning of tribulation. Light from fire = guidance and knowledge.
cultural_interpretations.hindu: Agni (fire) is sacred - the messenger between humans and gods. Fire in dreams represents tapas (spiritual heat/discipline), yajna (ritual sacrifice), and transformation. Dreaming of a sacred fire is auspicious and indicates spiritual progress.

follow_up_questions: Was the fire destructive or controlled?
Were you in danger or observing from safety?
What was burning - a building, nature, or something else?
How did you feel - scared, mesmerized, or empowered?
Is there something in your life that feels urgent or out of control?
Are you going through a major transformation?

psychological_interpretations.jungian: The creative process - something new gestating in the psyche. The gestation period = the time needed for psychic development. Giving birth = bringing a new aspect of self into consciousness. The nature of what's born reveals what's emerging.
psychological_interpretations.freudian: Literal wish for or anxiety about pregnancy. Also represents creative projects, ideas gestating, or the birth of a new identity. Can reflect anxieties about fertility and femininity/masculinity.
psychological_interpretations.cognitive: Creative incubation and project development. A new idea, plan, or phase of life is developing. The ease or difficulty of the birth reflects the dreamer's confidence about bringing it to fruition.

cultural_interpretations.western: Creativity, new projects, new beginnings, potential, anticipation. Not always literal pregnancy. Can represent any creative or developmental process nearing completion.
cultural_interpretations.chinese: 周公解梦: Dreaming of pregnancy (梦见怀孕) is very auspicious, indicating wealth, prosperity, and successful new ventures. Giving birth (生孩子) indicates the successful completion of a project or new opportunities. For unmarried women, it may indicate a new relationship.
cultural_interpretations.islamic: Pregnancy in dreams generally signifies increase - in wealth, knowledge, or blessings. Giving birth to a boy = relief from difficulties. Giving birth to a girl = ease and comfort. The gender and appearance of the child carry specific meanings.
cultural_interpretations.hindu: Pregnancy dreams are highly auspicious, representing the creative power of Shakti. They indicate new beginnings, prosperity, and the fruition of past efforts. In some traditions, they may indicate the soul of a future child seeking incarnation.

follow_up_questions: Are you actually pregnant or trying to conceive?
How did you feel about the pregnancy - excited, scared, or surprised?
Was the birth easy or difficult?
Is there a creative project or new venture developing in your life?
Who else was present in the dream?
What was born - a normal baby or something unusual?

psychological_interpretations.jungian: The unconscious is overwhelming the ego - repressed material is flooding consciousness. Can represent a necessary breakthrough where the rigid ego structure needs to be dissolved for growth. The flood is both destructive and renewing (cf. Noah, Deucalion).
psychological_interpretations.freudian: Being overwhelmed by repressed emotions, especially grief or rage. The force of the water represents the force of the id breaking through the ego's defenses.
psychological_interpretations.cognitive: Emotional overwhelm mapped to physical overwhelm. Reflects feeling inundated by work, emotions, responsibilities, or life changes. Threat simulation of a primal environmental danger.

cultural_interpretations.western: Emotional overwhelm, loss of control, cleansing/renewal, major life changes sweeping away the old. The response to the flood matters - fighting it vs. floating with it.
cultural_interpretations.chinese: 周公解梦: Floods (洪水) represent challenges and difficulties ahead, but also the potential for great change and new opportunities arising from destruction. Water is wealth, so abundant water can mean abundant fortune - if managed properly.
cultural_interpretations.islamic: Floods may represent trials and tribulations. However, surviving a flood indicates overcoming difficulty with divine help. The flood of Nuh (Noah) is the archetypal example - destruction of the sinful and preservation of the faithful.
cultural_interpretations.hindu: Floods connect to the cosmic dissolution (pralaya) at the end of each age. They represent the clearing away of old karma to make room for new creation. Lord Vishnu resting on the cosmic ocean between ages is the archetypal image.

follow_up_questions: Were you caught in the flood or watching from safety?
How fast was the water rising?
Were you able to escape or swim?
Is there something in your life that feels overwhelming or out of control?
Was anyone else affected by the flood?
What was the aftermath - destruction or renewal?

psychological_interpretations.jungian: Dogs represent loyalty, instinct, and the faithful companion aspect of the psyche. A loyal dog = a healthy relationship with one's instincts. An aggressive dog = instincts that have been suppressed and are now hostile. The black dog = depression (Churchill's metaphor).
psychological_interpretations.freudian: Animals represent the id and instinctual drives. Dogs specifically represent loyalty conflicts, obedience vs. rebellion, and domesticated vs. wild impulses.
psychological_interpretations.cognitive: Reflects relationships, trust, loyalty, companionship, and protection. Aggressive dogs map to perceived threats from people the dreamer trusted. Continuity from waking-life pet relationships or encounters.

cultural_interpretations.western: Loyalty, friendship, protection, trust. A friendly dog = reliable relationships. An attacking dog = betrayal of trust. Man's best friend reflects the dreamer's closest relationships.
cultural_interpretations.chinese: 周公解梦: Dogs (狗) in dreams generally indicate loyalty and friendship. A dog barking at you = gossip or slander from others. A dog biting = betrayal by a friend. A gentle dog = a faithful friend will help you.
cultural_interpretations.islamic: In Islamic tradition, dogs have complex symbolism. A guard dog = protection. A barking dog = a hostile person. A dog biting = harm from an enemy. Dreaming of hunting with a dog = beneficial partnership.
cultural_interpretations.hindu: Dogs are associated with Bhairava (fierce form of Shiva) and are guardians of the afterworld. A dog following you may indicate loyalty or protection. In the Mahabharata, a dog follows the Pandavas on their final journey, representing dharma itself.

follow_up_questions: Was the dog friendly or aggressive?
Was it a dog you recognize, or a strange dog?
What breed or size? What color?
Did the dog interact with you or others?
Is there someone in your life you associate with the qualities of this dog?
How is trust and loyalty showing up in your relationships right now?

psychological_interpretations.jungian: The spider as the Great Mother archetype - the weaver of fate and destiny. The web represents the interconnected pattern of life. Can represent the devouring mother or the creative feminine. Arachne mythology: creativity and hubris.
psychological_interpretations.freudian: Often represents the overbearing mother figure. The web = feeling trapped in a relationship. The spider's patience = manipulation or passive aggression. Can also represent female sexuality and entrapment.
psychological_interpretations.cognitive: Evolutionary fear response (arachnophobia is partially innate). Psychologically, represents feeling trapped, manipulated, or overwhelmed by complexity. Also creativity and patience - the spider as master architect.

cultural_interpretations.western: Creativity, patience, being trapped, manipulation, feminine power, fear. The web = complex situation you're caught in. A spider weaving = creative work in progress.
cultural_interpretations.chinese: 周公解梦: Spiders (蜘蛛) can represent industriousness and good fortune. Seeing a spider web = a trap or complex situation. Killing a spider may indicate eliminating a problem. A spider descending = a visitor arriving.
cultural_interpretations.islamic: The spider holds a special place in Islam - a spider web protected the Prophet Muhammad in the cave of Thawr during the Hijra. Thus, spiders can represent divine protection. However, a spider's web can also represent fragility and the weakness of false beliefs.
cultural_interpretations.hindu: The spider (and its web) is used in the Upanishads as a metaphor for Brahman creating the universe - as a spider projects its web from itself and reabsorbs it. Dreaming of a spider may relate to creation, maya, and the fabric of reality.

follow_up_questions: How big was the spider? What did it look like?
Was it building a web or approaching you?
How did you feel - terrified, fascinated, or calm?
Is there a situation in your life that feels like a web or trap?
Do you have a particular fear of spiders in waking life?
Are you working on something creative or intricate?

psychological_interpretations.jungian: Birds represent the spirit, transcendence, and thoughts rising from the unconscious. Different birds carry different meanings: eagle = the Self; owl = wisdom of the unconscious; crow = the Shadow or trickster. A caged bird = trapped spirit or repressed thoughts.
psychological_interpretations.freudian: Phallic symbol (especially in flight). Also represents aspirations and ambitions. A caged bird = repressed desires. Free flight = liberated instincts.
psychological_interpretations.cognitive: Freedom, perspective, aspiration, communication (messenger birds). The type of bird and its behavior map to the dreamer's relationship with freedom and constraint. Reflects desire for broader perspective on life situations.

cultural_interpretations.western: Freedom, spirit, perspective, messages, aspirations. Eagles = power and vision. Doves = peace. Ravens = mystery or omens. A dead bird = lost opportunity or end of freedom.
cultural_interpretations.chinese: 周公解梦: Birds (鸟) generally represent good news and communication. A phoenix (凤凰) is extremely auspicious, representing rebirth and prosperity. A crow may warn of bad news. A magpie (喜鹊) brings joy and good fortune.
cultural_interpretations.islamic: Birds often represent travelers or messengers. A caged bird = imprisonment. A bird singing = good news. An eagle = a powerful ruler. Specific birds carry Quranic significance - the hoopoe (hudhud) carried messages for Prophet Sulayman.
cultural_interpretations.hindu: Birds are vahanas (vehicles) of the gods - Garuda (eagle) carries Vishnu, the peacock carries Kartikeya, the swan/hamsa carries Saraswati and represents the ability to discern truth from illusion. Each bird species has specific divine associations.

follow_up_questions: What kind of bird was it?
Was it flying freely or trapped?
Was it singing, silent, or calling out?
How did you feel watching or interacting with the bird?
Is there an area of your life where you feel caged or free?
Did the bird have a message or significance in the dream?

psychological_interpretations.jungian: The cat represents feminine independence, intuition, and the mysterious aspects of the Anima. Cats are associated with the moon, the unconscious, and autonomous femininity that resists domestication. A friendly cat = harmonious relationship with the feminine unconscious.
psychological_interpretations.freudian: Represents feminine sexuality, independence, and sensuality. Can also represent hidden aggression (retractable claws). The cat's nocturnal nature connects it to hidden desires.
psychological_interpretations.cognitive: Independence, intuition, self-sufficiency, mystery. Reflects the dreamer's relationship with autonomy and boundaries. Cats that scratch = boundary violations. Purring cats = comfort with independence.

cultural_interpretations.western: Independence, intuition, feminine energy, mystery, bad luck (black cats in some traditions) or good luck (others). A cat's behavior in the dream reflects the dreamer's relationship with independence and intuition.
cultural_interpretations.chinese: 周公解梦: Cats (猫) have mixed interpretations. A cat catching a mouse = overcoming enemies or difficulties. A black cat = potential bad luck. A cat scratching = gossip or conflict with a woman. A cat purring = domestic comfort.
cultural_interpretations.islamic: Cats are generally well-regarded in Islam (the Prophet was fond of cats). A clean, well-kept cat = blessings. A wild or feral cat = a thief or untrustworthy person. A cat in the home = comfort and companionship.
cultural_interpretations.hindu: Cats are associated with the goddess Shashthi, protector of children. In some traditions, dreaming of a cat may warn of deception or hidden enemies. A black cat crossing one's path (even in dreams) may indicate obstacles.

follow_up_questions: What was the cat doing - affectionate, aloof, or aggressive?
Was it your cat or a strange cat?
What color was the cat?
How did you feel about the cat?
Is there someone in your life who embodies cat-like qualities - independent, mysterious?
Are you trusting your intuition in a current situation?

common_variations: Trying to find shelter during earthquake

psychological_interpretations.jungian: The very foundation of the psyche is being shaken - core beliefs, identity, or fundamental assumptions are being challenged. The earthquake comes from below (the unconscious) and disrupts the surface (ego-consciousness). A necessary destabilization for reconstruction.
psychological_interpretations.freudian: Represents instability in the ego's foundation. Repressed material from the unconscious is erupting violently. The ground represents the basis of psychological stability.
psychological_interpretations.cognitive: Personal stability being shaken - relationship upheaval, career changes, worldview shifts, health crises. Maps to the visceral experience of having the ground pulled from under you. Threat simulation of environmental danger.

cultural_interpretations.western: Instability, upheaval, foundations shaking, major life changes that feel beyond control. Something you thought was stable is being disrupted.
cultural_interpretations.chinese: 周公解梦: Earthquakes (地震) warn of major changes or disruptions ahead. They may indicate shifts in career, family structure, or living situation. Surviving an earthquake = overcoming major difficulties.
cultural_interpretations.islamic: Earthquakes are signs of divine power (referenced in Surah Az-Zalzalah). They may represent a spiritual awakening, reckoning, or divine reminder. Surviving indicates divine mercy and protection.
cultural_interpretations.hindu: Earthquakes relate to cosmic cycles and the upheaval of established order. They may indicate the end of a yuga-like personal cycle. The ground represents prakriti (material nature) being disturbed.

follow_up_questions: How severe was the earthquake?
Were you inside a building or outside?
Did anything collapse around you?
What part of your life feels unstable right now?
Were you able to find safety?
Has anything recently shaken your beliefs or expectations?

psychological_interpretations.jungian: Money represents psychic energy, self-worth, and personal value. Finding money = discovering untapped resources within. Losing money = energy being drained or invested poorly. The amount represents the magnitude of the psychic investment.
psychological_interpretations.freudian: Freud associated money with feces (anal stage development) and control. Money represents power, control, self-worth, and retained vs. released energy.
psychological_interpretations.cognitive: Direct reflection of financial anxieties, career concerns, and self-worth. Finding money = unexpected gain or undervalued self. Losing money = financial stress or feeling undervalued.

cultural_interpretations.western: Self-worth, value, power, security, success. Finding money = recognizing your own value. Losing money = feeling devalued. The condition and amount of money reflect the dreamer's sense of abundance vs. scarcity.
cultural_interpretations.chinese: 周公解梦: Money (钱) dreams are very specifically interpreted. Finding money = good luck ahead. Losing money = avoid risky investments. Giving money away = generosity bringing returns. Receiving gold = great prosperity.
cultural_interpretations.islamic: Money in dreams can represent knowledge, faith, or worldly gain depending on context. Finding money = receiving knowledge or provision. Losing money = losing knowledge or faith. Counterfeit money = hypocrisy or deception.
cultural_interpretations.hindu: Lakshmi, goddess of wealth, may manifest through money dreams. Finding money indicates her blessings. Losing money may indicate a need for more generosity (dana) to invite abundance.

follow_up_questions: Were you finding, losing, or receiving money?
How much money was involved?
How did you feel about the money - excited, anxious, or guilty?
Are you concerned about finances in waking life?
Do you feel valued and adequately compensated?
What does money represent to you beyond its material value?

common_variations: Looking in mirror and seeing different face
Mirror showing the future or past

psychological_interpretations.jungian: The mirror represents self-reflection and confrontation with the true Self vs. the Persona. A distorted reflection = dissonance between who you are and who you think you are. No reflection = loss of identity or ego dissolution.
psychological_interpretations.freudian: Narcissistic self-examination. The mirror reflects the dreamer's relationship with their own image and self-concept. A broken mirror = fragmented self-image.
psychological_interpretations.cognitive: Self-perception and identity processing. The brain processes self-concept during sleep. A different face = identity in flux. No reflection = feeling invisible or losing sense of self.

cultural_interpretations.western: Self-knowledge, vanity, truth, identity. A clear mirror = honest self-assessment. A broken mirror = superstition aside, represents fractured self-image or bad luck in some traditions.
cultural_interpretations.chinese: 周公解梦: Mirrors (镜子) represent truth and self-knowledge. A clear mirror = clarity about yourself. A broken mirror (破镜) traditionally represents separation from a loved one. Looking in a mirror and seeing someone else = identity confusion.
cultural_interpretations.islamic: A mirror represents self-reflection and truth. Seeing a clear reflection = self-awareness and clarity. A dirty mirror = the heart clouded by sin. Mirrors can also represent one's spouse or companion.
cultural_interpretations.hindu: The mirror represents maya - the world of appearances vs. ultimate reality (Brahman). Vedanta philosophy uses the mirror metaphor extensively. A clear mirror = the purified mind reflecting truth.

follow_up_questions: What did you see in the mirror?
Was the reflection accurate, distorted, or completely different?
How did you feel seeing your reflection?
Are you going through an identity change or self-reassessment?
Was the mirror clear, cracked, or foggy?
Is there a gap between who you present to the world and who you feel you are?

symbols.name_zh: 楼梯 / 攀爬 / 下降

psychological_interpretations.jungian: Ascending = moving toward consciousness, spirit, and the Self. Descending = entering the unconscious, Shadow work, depth exploration. The staircase connects different levels of the psyche. Spiral stairs suggest the circuitous nature of individuation.
psychological_interpretations.freudian: Climbing stairs = sexual intercourse (rhythmic ascent). Ascending = social ambition. Descending = regression or exploring repressed material. The staircase connects the conscious (upper) to unconscious (lower) mind.
psychological_interpretations.cognitive: Progress (or lack thereof) toward goals. Endless stairs = feeling like you're working hard but not advancing. Reaching the top = achievement. Descending = going deeper into a problem or facing decline.

cultural_interpretations.western: Ambition, progress, spiritual ascent, career advancement. Climbing = striving for goals. Descending = introspection or decline. The difficulty of the climb reflects the difficulty of the goal.
cultural_interpretations.chinese: 周公解梦: Climbing stairs (爬楼梯) upward = career advancement and rising status. Going down = potential setbacks. An endless staircase = a goal that requires persistence.
cultural_interpretations.islamic: Ascending stairs = rising in faith, knowledge, or worldly station. Descending = moving away from righteousness. A staircase to the sky = spiritual elevation. Steps represent stages of spiritual progress.
cultural_interpretations.hindu: The seven chakras are often depicted as ascending stages. Climbing represents spiritual evolution through the chakra system. The summit (Sahasrara) represents enlightenment and union with the divine.

follow_up_questions: Were you going up or down?
Was the climb easy or difficult?
Did you reach your destination?
What was at the top or bottom?
Is there a goal you're working toward that feels like an uphill climb?
Were the stairs well-built or crumbling?

psychological_interpretations.jungian: The sacred marriage (hieros gamos) - the union of opposites within the psyche, particularly Anima/Animus integration. A wedding symbolizes achieving wholeness through the union of conscious and unconscious elements. A failed wedding = integration is being resisted.
psychological_interpretations.freudian: Desire for commitment, fear of commitment, or anxiety about a relationship. The wedding partner represents the desired or feared merger. Wedding anxieties reflect intimacy fears.
psychological_interpretations.cognitive: Reflects real relationship concerns, desires for partnership, or commitment anxieties. For engaged/married people, often processes wedding-related stress. Can also represent any major commitment or merger (business, project).

cultural_interpretations.western: Commitment, partnership, union, transition, anxiety about change. Wedding dreams before an actual wedding are extremely common and process anxieties about the life change.
cultural_interpretations.chinese: 周公解梦: Wedding dreams (结婚) are generally auspicious, indicating new partnerships, prosperity, and harmony. However, dreaming of your own wedding may paradoxically indicate sadness or challenges ahead. Attending another's wedding = good news.
cultural_interpretations.islamic: Marriage in dreams often represents a covenant or contract. Marrying a known person = forming an alliance. A joyful wedding = blessings. Marriage also symbolizes the relationship between the believer and their faith.
cultural_interpretations.hindu: Marriage (vivaha) is one of the sixteen samskaras (sacraments). Dreaming of marriage indicates union, completeness, and dharmic partnership. The specific wedding rituals in the dream carry significance.

follow_up_questions: Were you the one getting married?
Who was the partner?
Was the wedding joyful or anxiety-filled?
Are you in a relationship? Is commitment a current theme?
Did anything go wrong?
Is there a major commitment or partnership forming in any area of your life?

psychological_interpretations.jungian: The bridge is a transitional symbol connecting two states of consciousness, two life phases, or the conscious and unconscious. Crossing = successful transition. A collapsing bridge = the transition is failing or the dreamer is not ready.
psychological_interpretations.freudian: Represents transition between psychological states. The bridge's stability reflects the ego's ability to navigate change. Fear of crossing = fear of change.
psychological_interpretations.cognitive: Transition, decision points, connection between phases of life. A bridge connects where you are to where you need to be. Its condition reflects your confidence in the transition ahead.

cultural_interpretations.western: Transition, overcoming obstacles, connection, moving from one phase to another. 'Burning bridges' = cutting off the past.
cultural_interpretations.chinese: 周公解梦: Bridges (桥) represent transitions and opportunities. Crossing a bridge successfully = overcoming obstacles. A broken bridge = blocked path requiring alternative approaches. A stone bridge = reliable support.
cultural_interpretations.islamic: The bridge (sirat) has profound eschatological significance - the bridge over hellfire that all must cross on Judgment Day. A bridge in dreams may represent a test of faith or the path between this world and the next.
cultural_interpretations.hindu: The bridge between the material world and the spiritual realm. In the Ramayana, Lord Rama built the bridge (Rama Setu) to Lanka - representing the conquest of obstacles through devotion and determination.

follow_up_questions: Were you crossing the bridge or standing at the edge?
What was the bridge over - water, a void, a valley?
Was the bridge sturdy or fragile?
Is there a transition or decision you're facing in waking life?
What was on the other side?
Were you afraid to cross or eager?

symbols.name_zh: 丢失手机 / 无法打电话
common_variations: Phone battery dying at critical moment
Phone screen won't respond to touch

psychological_interpretations.jungian: Modern manifestation of communication breakdown with the Self. The phone represents the dreamer's ability to connect with others and with their own unconscious. Loss of phone = loss of psychic connection.
psychological_interpretations.freudian: Communication breakdown, isolation anxiety. The phone represents the ability to reach out and be reached. Loss represents fear of disconnection or inability to express needs.
psychological_interpretations.cognitive: Reflects technology dependence anxiety and communication concerns. The phone is the modern extension of self and social connection. Loss represents disconnection, missing information, or inability to reach help when needed.

cultural_interpretations.western: Disconnection, communication breakdown, isolation, missing information, social anxiety. Very common in digital age. Reflects real dependency on devices for identity and connection.
cultural_interpretations.chinese: Modern interpretations: Losing a phone represents losing connection with important people or missing opportunities. A broken phone = communication difficulties in relationships.
cultural_interpretations.islamic: Modern scholars relate phone dreams to communication with others and with Allah. Being unable to call = barriers in prayer or connection. The phone is analogous to the heart as a receiver of divine communication.
cultural_interpretations.hindu: Modern interpretation: The phone represents maya's web of communication. Losing it may paradoxically indicate liberation from worldly attachment, or it may indicate loss of connection with one's community.

follow_up_questions: Who were you trying to contact?
What was wrong with the phone?
How urgently did you need to make the call?
Is there someone you're struggling to reach or connect with?
Do you feel disconnected from your social circle?
Is there an important message you need to deliver but can't?

psychological_interpretations.jungian: The ego's willpower is insufficient against the power of the situation. The dreamer's conscious efforts are being overwhelmed by unconscious forces. The body refusing to obey the mind represents the psyche's rejection of the ego's demands.
psychological_interpretations.freudian: The body's resistance represents internal conflict - part of the psyche wants to flee while another part holds back. The paralysis between desire and prohibition creates the frustrating middle ground.
psychological_interpretations.cognitive: During REM sleep, voluntary muscles are inhibited (atonia). The brain interprets this physical paralysis narratively as inability to run. Psychologically reflects feelings of helplessness, inadequacy, or being unprepared to face a challenge.

cultural_interpretations.western: Helplessness, frustration, inadequacy. Feeling unable to escape a situation or meet a challenge. The harder you try, the worse it gets - mirroring waking life situations where effort seems futile.
cultural_interpretations.chinese: 周公解梦: Being unable to run (跑不动) indicates obstacles and resistance in current endeavors. It suggests the need for patience and alternative approaches rather than brute force.
cultural_interpretations.islamic: Inability to move or run may indicate spiritual lethargy or being held back by worldly concerns. It can serve as a reminder to seek divine assistance rather than relying solely on personal effort.
cultural_interpretations.hindu: Relates to the concept of prarabdha karma - past actions creating present obstacles. The inability to run suggests karmic bonds that must be worked through, not escaped.

follow_up_questions: Were you running from something or toward something?
What was making it hard to run - heavy legs, sticky ground, or something else?
How did you feel - frustrated, terrified, or resigned?
Is there a situation in your life where you feel powerless or stuck?
Are your efforts in some area of life not producing results?
What happened when you couldn't run - were you caught?

psychological_interpretations.jungian: The 'other person' often represents the Anima/Animus - the inner counterpart seeking union. A partner cheating may reflect the dreamer's anxiety that their partner's psychic energy is being directed elsewhere. Self-cheating = exploring forbidden aspects of identity.
psychological_interpretations.freudian: Wish fulfillment or anxiety about desire. Dreaming of one's own infidelity = repressed desires for novelty or forbidden partners. Partner cheating = projection of one's own desires onto the partner, or genuine insecurity.
psychological_interpretations.cognitive: Reflects relationship insecurities, trust issues, fear of abandonment, or jealousy. Can also reflect emotional neglect - the dreamer feels their partner's attention is elsewhere (work, friends, hobbies). Usually more about emotional security than literal infidelity.

cultural_interpretations.western: Trust, insecurity, fear of abandonment, jealousy, self-worth in relationships. Very common even in healthy relationships. Often triggered by periods of emotional distance.
cultural_interpretations.chinese: 周公解梦: Dreams of infidelity (出轨) may paradoxically indicate a strong bond - the anxiety reflects how much the relationship matters. It may also serve as a warning to invest more attention in the relationship.
cultural_interpretations.islamic: Dreams of marital infidelity may indicate Shaytan sowing discord between spouses. Islamic guidance recommends seeking refuge in Allah and not sharing such dreams with the spouse, as they may be mere whispers (waswasa).
cultural_interpretations.hindu: Infidelity dreams may relate to the concept of attachment (raga) and the fear of loss. They can indicate the need to strengthen the bond of trust (vishwas) in the relationship.

follow_up_questions: Were you the one cheating or was your partner?
Who was the third person? Someone you know?
How did you feel - angry, guilty, relieved, or devastated?
Is there any distance or tension in your current relationship?
Have there been trust issues in the past?
Do you feel emotionally fulfilled in your relationship?

common_variations: Using the toilet and it feels real

psychological_interpretations.jungian: The toilet represents the shadow's processing station - where psychic waste is released. Inability to find one = inability to release or process negative emotions. No privacy = feeling exposed in vulnerability. A dirty toilet = contaminated emotional processing.
psychological_interpretations.freudian: Central to anal stage psychology. Represents control, release, letting go, and bodily functions. A clogged toilet = emotional constipation. Can't find a toilet = resistance to letting go of control.
psychological_interpretations.cognitive: Often has a physiological component - the body needs to urinate and the brain creates a scenario around this need. Psychologically represents the need to release emotions, let go of negativity, or address 'dirty' issues in private.

cultural_interpretations.western: Privacy needs, letting go, release of negative emotions, basic needs being unmet. The public nature of dream toilets often reflects vulnerability about private matters becoming public.
cultural_interpretations.chinese: 周公解梦: Dreaming of a toilet (厕所) can indicate the need to cleanse or release something negative. A clean toilet = upcoming resolution of problems. A dirty toilet = need to address unpleasant matters you've been avoiding.
cultural_interpretations.islamic: Toilets in dreams relate to purification (taharah). A clean bathroom = spiritual cleanliness. A dirty bathroom = need for spiritual purification. The need for privacy reflects the importance of modesty (haya).
cultural_interpretations.hindu: Relates to the concept of shuddhi (purification) and the release of impurities. The bathroom represents the necessary process of letting go of what no longer serves - both physically and spiritually.

follow_up_questions: What was the condition of the toilet?
Were you looking for one, or was the issue about privacy?
Did you actually need to go to the bathroom in real life?
Is there something you need to release or let go of?
Is there a private matter you're afraid of becoming public?
How did you feel - desperate, embarrassed, or disgusted?

psychological_interpretations.jungian: Being overwhelmed by the unconscious. The ego is losing its boundary and being dissolved by the waters of the unconscious. If the dreamer survives, it represents successfully navigating the depths. If drowning continues, the ego is overwhelmed.
psychological_interpretations.freudian: Overwhelming anxiety, fear of dissolution, return to prenatal state (water = womb). Being submerged = being consumed by repressed emotions.
psychological_interpretations.cognitive: Emotional overwhelm - literally 'drowning' in feelings, responsibilities, or circumstances. Reflects waking feelings of being in over your head. The ability to breathe underwater indicates adaptive coping.

cultural_interpretations.western: Being overwhelmed, in over your head, emotional flooding, losing control. Surviving drowning = overcoming seemingly impossible odds.
cultural_interpretations.chinese: 周公解梦: Drowning (溺水) warns of potential dangers, especially financial or emotional. Being rescued from drowning = help will come from unexpected sources. Watching someone drown = a relationship needs attention.
cultural_interpretations.islamic: Drowning may represent being overwhelmed by worldly affairs or debt. Being rescued = divine intervention. Drowning in clear water = immersion in faith. Drowning in muddy water = falling into fitna (trials).
cultural_interpretations.hindu: Drowning represents being caught in the ocean of samsara (cycle of birth and death). The rescue represents moksha (liberation) or the grace of a guru pulling one from the waters of illusion.

follow_up_questions: What type of water were you drowning in?
Were you alone or was someone else there?
Did someone rescue you, or did you save yourself?
What area of your life feels overwhelming right now?
Could you breathe underwater at any point?
How did you feel during the drowning - terrified, accepting, or fighting?

symbols.name_zh: 战争 / 战斗 / 暴力

psychological_interpretations.jungian: Inner conflict between opposing psychic forces. The 'enemies' represent disowned parts of the self. War represents the battle between ego and Shadow, or between competing archetypal demands. Resolution comes through integration, not victory.
psychological_interpretations.freudian: Aggression drive (Thanatos) finding expression. Repressed anger, hostility, or territorial instincts surfacing. The targets of violence represent objects of unconscious hostility.
psychological_interpretations.cognitive: Threat simulation at its most extreme. Reflects interpersonal conflict, workplace politics, competitive situations, or internal moral conflicts. Can be triggered by violent media consumption or real-world conflict exposure.

cultural_interpretations.western: Conflict, aggression, competition, inner turmoil, survival instincts. The nature of the conflict and your role in it (aggressor, defender, bystander) reveals your relationship to conflict in waking life.
